tweets.title:
What happened in Moscow today reinforced a thought I’ve had for some time.

Our appetite for taking losses and enduring disruptions is relatively low as a society.

A few successful strikes, airport closures, disruptions to daily life, and relentless media coverage could generate so much public pressure that the government’s room for military response starts shrinking.

In the drone age, deterrence is not just about the ability to inflict pain. It’s also about the ability to absorb it. Are we ready as a society??

• Strait of Hormuz traffic normalizes
• Middle Eastern oil flows resume
• Iranian crude re-enters global markets
• Import diversification continues

Russia is expected to remain an important supplier, though sanctioned entities could face renewed restrictions.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: What Iran Gains ?

Sanctions Relief: The US commits to a schedule for terminating unilateral, UN Security Council, and IAEA sanctions, including issuing waivers to resume Iranian crude oil exports and associated banking services.

Access to Frozen Assets: Restricted Iranian funds will be released for use by Iran's central bank upon implementation.

$300 Billion Reconstruction Plan: The US and regional partners plan to develop a minimum $300 billion economic development package for Iran, to be finalized within 60 days.

Removal of Naval Blockade: The US will lift its naval blockade within 30 days, restoring pre-war shipping traffic, and withdraw military forces from Iran's proximity within 30 days of a final deal.

India is considering a proposal to require refiners to maintain significantly larger crude oil inventories, taking cues from China’s stockpiling model.

The move comes after disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz exposed vulnerabilities in India's energy supply chain.

Current strategic reserves: ~21 million barrels
China: ~1.4 billion barrels

If implemented, refiners may be asked to hold enough crude to cover up to 30 days of national consumption, potentially requiring investments worth tens of thousands of crores in oil purchases and storage infrastructure.

tweets.title:
A scramjet cannot operate from a standstill. It first needs a powerful booster to accelerate the vehicle to very high supersonic/hypersonic speeds before the scramjet can ignite.

That is why HSTDV was mounted on an Agni-derived booster, which accelerated the vehicle to the required speed and altitude before the scramjet took over. The same principle is used in other hypersonic programmes worldwide, including Russia’s Zircon, where a rocket booster brings the missile into the scramjet’s operating envelope.

The concept of encapsulating a scramjet vehicle inside a booster and triggering the engine only after the required speed and flight conditions are achieved is not easy to grasp for those new to defence technology.

Image @Kuntal__biswas : Thank you

BREAKING: Delhi Police Dismantles ISI-Linked Terror Module

In a major counter-terror operation, Delhi Police Special Cell has arrested 5 more operatives, taking the total arrests to 8, and dismantling a network allegedly run by Pakistan-based handler Shahzad Bhatti.

According to investigators, members of Tehreek-e-Taliban Hindustan (TTH) were tasked with targeting police personnel, spreading pro-Pakistan propaganda, and promoting the outfit through posters and graffiti across Delhi-NCR.

Arms, ammunition, digital devices, and incriminating material have been seized. Authorities believe the network was part of a larger cross-border terror conspiracy.

The crackdown comes as the NIA conducts raids across Punjab and Haryana to uncover the full extent of the alleged Pakistan-linked terror network.

DRDO is developing an indigenous Active Protection System (APS) for the Indian Army's T-90 tanks.

The system is designed to counter anti-tank missiles, drones, loitering munitions, and top-attack weapons through a combination of hard-kill and soft-kill technologies.

According to DRDO documents, the APS aims to intercept threats travelling at speeds exceeding 1,500 m/s, significantly enhancing the survivability of India's armoured fleet.

If successful, it will reduce dependence on foreign tank protection systems.
